Title: Negative Regulation of Anthocyanin Biosynthesis in Arabidopsis by a miR156-Targeted SPL Transcription Factor

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Anthocyanin branch competing with flavonol branch
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces dihydroflavonols such as dihydrokaempferol to leucoanthocyanidins, directing flux toward anthocyanin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pigmentation and potential defense against herbivores; general protective secondary metabolites
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Stem base/junction between rosette and stem; hypocotyl-cotyledon junction
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms immediate precursor of target product and determines flux into anthocyanin branch
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern: Highest in basal stem region, lowest in apical stem; promoter active at junction between stem and rosette leaves
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Origin_Species: Highest in basal stem region, lowest in apical stem
Pathway_Genes.Promoters: Native DFR promoter; ProDFR:GUS reporter; ProDFR:GUS mu with mutated GTAC boxes
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: SPL9; PAP1; TT8; TTG1 complex context
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Long-day growth; dexamethasone treatment in ProSPL9:rSPL9-GR seedlings; cycloheximide cotreatment for direct regulation test
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point/branch competition step supported by expression and flux analyses; not explicitly designated rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced DFR activity is associated with altered flux toward flavonols/quercetin rather than anthocyanins
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: qRT-PCR; ProDFR:GUS reporter; DEX-inducible rSPL9-GR assay with CHX; ChIP-qPCR
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DFR transcript levels strongly increased in miR156 overexpression plants and decreased in MIM156 plants. 2. DFR shows an inverse spatial expression pattern to SPL9/SPL15 along the stem. 3. SPL9 is recruited to the DFR promoter and rapidly represses DFR expression. 4. DFR is a major branch-control node linking SPL activity to anthocyanin accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Higher DFR expression is associated with increased anthocyanin accumulation; SPL-mediated repression of DFR reduces anthocyanins and favors flavonol accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mutation of GTAC boxes in the DFR promoter did not obviously change reporter pattern, suggesting SPL9 is indirectly recruited to the promoter rather than acting through canonical GTAC motifs alone
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating DFR together with SPL/miR156 regulators could redirect flavonoid flux to tune anthocyanin versus flavonol content in crops

Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: SQUAMOSA PROMOTER BINDING PROTEIN-LIKE (SBP/SPL) transcription factor with SBP DNA-binding domain
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional repression of anthocyanin biosynthetic output through destabilization of an activator complex; indirect promoter recruitment
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: DFR; ANS; F3'H; UGT75C1; PAP1-containing MYB-bHLH-WD40 complex
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Represses DFR and other anthocyanin biosynthetic genes; competes with TT8 for PAP1 binding and destabilizes the MYB-bHLH-WD40 activation complex
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Anthocyanin biosynthesis and flavonoid branch flux allocation between anthocyanins and flavonols
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Anthocyanins provide pigmentation and possible defense; flavonols are major flavonoid secondary metabolites
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Stem-rosette junction; hypocotyl-cotyledon junction; inflorescence internodes
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern: Higher in apical than basal stem, opposite to DFR; activity increases as plants progress developmentally toward flowering
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Origin_Species: Higher in apical stem region and later developmental phases
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: miR156 negatively regulates SPL9; developmental age/floral transition; dexamethasone in rSPL9-GR experimental system
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Potential indirect feedback within flavonoid pathway inferred; no direct SPL9 feedback loop conclusively shown
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Interferes with MYB-bHLH-WD40 transcriptional activation complex by binding PAP1 and competing with TT8
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Long-day growth; DEX induction; CHX cotreatment; transient assays in Nicotiana benthamiana
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Overexpression analysis; MIM156 and MIR156 genetic modulation; DEX-inducible rSPL9-GR assay; ChIP-qPCR; yeast two-hybrid; BiFC/LUC; coimmunoprecipitation; transient competition assay
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: SPL9 suppresses anthocyanin accumulation and promotes a shift toward flavonol accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent major negative regulator linking developmental phase transition to anthocyanin/flavonol flux balance
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Overexpression of SPL9 reduces anthocyanin accumulation. 2. SPL9 directly associates with the DFR promoter and rapidly represses DFR transcription. 3. SPL9 binds PAP1 and competes with TT8, destabilizing the MYB-bHLH-WD40 complex. 4. SPL9 thereby negatively regulates anthocyanin biosynthetic gene expression and shifts flux toward flavonols.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although SPL proteins are often activators, SPL9 acts here as a bifunctional regulator with repressive output on anthocyanin genes via protein-protein interaction rather than direct GTAC-box-dependent activation/repression
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A strong engineering target for tuning anthocyanin/flavonol ratio; reducing SPL9 activity or increasing miR156 may enhance anthocyanin accumulation, but developmental effects on phase change/flowering should be considered

Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: SPL9-type SPL genes including SPL9 and SPL10; broadly miR156-targeted SPL genes
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Reduces SPL activity, thereby indirectly increasing DFR and other anthocyanin biosynthetic genes while reducing flavonol accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Flavonoid branch regulation via upstream control of SPL transcription factors
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Stem-rosette junction; hypocotyl-cotyledon junction; stem tissues
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern: High miR156 activity correlates with high anthocyanin accumulation and low SPL activity; declines with developmental progression
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Origin_Species: High in earlier developmental stages; declines with age
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overexpression (Pro35S:MIR156) and target mimic inhibition (Pro35S:MIM156) with pigment/metabolite quantification
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Increased miR156 promotes anthocyanin accumulation and reduces kaempferol-type flavonols; reduced miR156 has the opposite effect
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Upstream developmental regulator of anthocyanin/flavonol ratio through SPL repression
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Elevated miR156 causes hyperaccumulation of anthocyanins in stems. 2. Reduced miR156 activity lowers anthocyanin but raises kaempferol derivatives. 3. miR156 controls flavonoid flux by repressing SPL genes, especially SPL9-type SPLs.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper frames miR156 as an upstream regulator, but mechanistic protein-level dissection centers primarily on SPL9
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating miR156 is a feasible route to increase anthocyanins, but pleiotropic effects on phase transition, flowering, leaf traits, and architecture are expected
